title: Split _hooklib.py — over-consumed god module on the enforcement path
evidence: _hooklib.py is 1,201 LOC with 46 top-level functions and is imported by 28 of the 44 core/pysrc modules — nearly triple the >10-consumer 'critical shared dependency' threshold in ai-markers.md. It bundles unrelated concerns: activation/frontmatter parsing (frontmatter_enabled_text, the canonical parser named by core/activation-contract.json), secret/crypto regexes (SECRET_RE, CRYPTO_RE), audit/gate-marker constants, host seam (get_host/set_host), digests (content_digest/line_digest), stdin plumbing (read_input, utf8_stdio), and block/exit semantics. Any edit to one concern ships risk to all 28 consumers on all 3 hosts; it is also the branch's highest-churn module (14 changes since 06-20).
impact: Maximal blast radius: a single-concern change forces re-verification of 28 modules across ca, ca-codex, and ca-pi; the module's grab-bag shape invites further accretion (each new gate adds constants/helpers here by default).
recommendation: Partition along the existing concern seams into focused libs (e.g. _activationlib for the contract parser, _sensitivelib for SECRET_RE/CRYPTO_RE/scan helpers, keeping _hooklib as the thin block/read_input/host-seam core), preserving the activation-contract.json canonicalParser pointer. No behavior change; imports rewire mechanically.
acceptance_criteria: no single _*lib.py imported by more than ~15 core modules
activation parser location updated in core/activation-contract.json canonicalParser
byte-identity CI still green across all three vendored copies
